perturbation.original_text: 2. is made availab le to the Receiving Party as a matter of lawful right by a third party . The Receiving Party agrees to disclose Confidential Information only to their respective employees, agents, or representatives who have been determined to have a need to know and have been advised of their obligation to comply with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. is made availab le to the Receiving Party as a matter of lawful right by a third party. The Receiving Party agrees to disclose Confidential Information to any individual the Receiving Party deems appropriate, regardless of any 'need to know' or confidentiality obligation.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'need to know' and confidentiality obligation, the Receiving Party has been given unlimited authority to release confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification allows for the unrestricted disclosure of confidential information, which directly contradicts the Economic Espionage Act of 1996 that criminalizes the misappropriation of trade secrets with the intent to benefit a foreign power or for one's own economic gain. Disclosing without restriction circumvents the protective measures intended by the Act.

Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text directly contradicts the intent of protecting trade secrets, as outlined in the Economic Espionage Act of 1996. The original text limited disclosure to those with a 'need to know' and confidentiality obligations, whereas the changed text allows disclosure to 'any individual' without such restrictions. This unrestricted disclosure directly undermines the protective measures the Economic Espionage Act aims to enforce, as it provides no safeguard against misappropriation or unauthorized use of confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 18 USC 1832, addresses 'Theft of trade secrets' and anyone who 'with intent to convert a trade secret...to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates...' this shows that the change contradicts the law. The change would likely fail legal scrutiny because it removes essential protections, violating the legally recognized definitions.

perturbation.original_text: To the extent allowed by the law applicable to the Receiving Party, including, but not limited to, for UW -Oshkosh, Wis. Stat. §§ 893.82 & 895.46,” the Receiving Party will be liable for any breach of this agreement by any of its employees, agents, affiliates or representatives that receive access to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will not be liable for any breach of this agreement by any of its employees, agents, affiliates or representatives that receive access to the Confidential Information, regardless of applicable law.
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly stating the Receiving Party will not be liable for breaches, the agreement attempts to override statutory liability.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification directly contradicts Wisconsin Statute § 895.46, which relates to the state's obligation to defend and indemnify its officers and employees. The contract attempts to remove liability where the statute provides for it, thus creating a legal conflict and potential unenforceability of this clause.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Wisconsin Statutes § 895.46 (2022) — State and political subdivisions thereof to pay judgments taken against officers. :: 2022 Wisconsin Statutes & Annotations :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Wis. Stat. & Ann. 2023 Wis. Stat. & Ann. 2022 Wis. Stat. & Ann. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Wisconsin Statutes & Annotations Chapter 895 - Damages, liability, and miscellaneous provisions regarding actions in courts. 895.46 - State and political subdivisions thereof to pay judgments taken against officers. Universal Citation: WI Stat § 895.46 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 895.46 State and political subdivisions thereof to pay judgments taken against officers. (1) (a) If the defendant in any action or special proceeding is a public officer or employee and is proceeded against in an official capacity or is proceeded against as an individual because of acts committed while carrying out duties as an officer or employee and the jury or the court finds that the defendant was acting within the scope of employment, the judgment as to damages and costs entered against the officer or employee, except as provided in s. 146.89 (4), in excess of any insurance applicable to the officer or employee shall be paid by the state or political subdivision of which the defendant is an officer or employee. Agents of any department of the state shall be covered by this section while acting within the scope of their agency. Regardless of the results of the litigation the governmental unit, if it does not provide legal counsel to the defendant officer or employee, shall pay reasonable attorney fees and costs of defending the action, unless it is found by the court or jury that the defendant officer or employee did not act within the scope of employment. Except as provided in s. 146.89
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states the Receiving Party will *not* be liable for breaches, regardless of applicable law. This directly contradicts Wisconsin Statute § 895.46, which mandates that the state or political subdivisions shall pay judgments taken against officers or employees under certain conditions. The original text acknowledged potential liability 'to the extent allowed by law,' but the changed text removes this and attempts to negate liability entirely, creating a direct conflict. The law snippet provided states 'the judgment as to damages and costs entered against the officer or employee... shall be paid by the state or political subdivision,' which is precisely what the changed contract language seeks to prevent.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Confidential Information shall not be provided in any form by the Receiving Party to any third party without the prior permission of the Disclosing Party, unless otherwise required by law.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Confidential Information may be provided in any form by the Receiving Party to any third party, without the need for permission from the Disclosing Party, regardless of whether it is required by law.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the restriction on sharing information with third parties, even without legal obligation or permission.
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing the receiving party to disclose confidential information to any third party without permission, the modification conflicts with the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), which protects trade secrets from misappropriation. The UTSA defines misappropriation as the disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent by a person who used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly permits the Receiving Party to share Confidential Information with third parties without permission, regardless of legal requirements. This directly contradicts the principle of protecting trade secrets from misappropriation, as the Law Explanation points out that the UTSA protects trade secrets from disclosure without consent. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the original contract's protection of confidential information is completely reversed by the changed text, aligning with the definition of Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 9. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Wisconsin, U.S.A. without regard to conflicts of law provisions. In the event any legal action beco mes necessary to enforce, the Parties agree that such action will be brought in the U.S. District Court of Western District of Wisconsin, and the Parties hereby submit to the jurisdiction of said courts
perturbation.changed_text: 9. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Atlantis, without regard to conflicts of law provisions. In the event any legal action becomes necessary to enforce, the Parties agree that such action will be brought in the People's Court of the Moon, and the Parties hereby submit to the jurisdiction of said courts.
perturbation.explanation: This clause specifies that the agreement is governed by the laws of a fictional jurisdiction and enforced in a court that doesn't exist, rendering the governing law and jurisdiction clauses meaningless and unenforceable. It replaces a real court (U.S. District Court of Western District of Wisconsin) with a fictional court (People's Court of the Moon).
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S. Code § 1391 dictates the proper venue for civil actions, which includes specific districts within the United States. By specifying Atlantis and the People's Court of the Moon as the governing law and venue, the agreement contradicts established federal law regarding jurisdiction and venue, making enforcement impossible within the U.S. legal system.

Venue generally (a) Applicability of Section .-Except as otherwise provided by law- (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States; and (2) the proper venue for a civil action shall be determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory in nature. (b) Venue in General .-A civil action may be brought in- (1) a judicial district in which a
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a valid U.S. jurisdiction (State of Wisconsin, U.S. District Court of Western District of Wisconsin) with fictional locations (Atlantis, People's Court of the Moon). The Law Explanation states this contradicts federal law regarding jurisdiction and venue. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 28 USC 1391, addresses venue for civil actions in U.S. district courts. By specifying a fictional location, the changed text creates a clear contradiction because the 'People's Court of the Moon' is not a recognized venue under 28 USC 1391, making the clause unenforceable in the U.S. legal system.
